question: Explain the basic read rule in Timestamp Ordering protocol with an example.
expectedPoints: Read rule: Transaction T can read data item Q only if TS(T) >= WTS(Q)
If condition fails, transaction is rolled back
Example: If transaction with timestamp 10 tries to read data with WTS=15, read is rejected because 10<15

question: What is Thomas' Write Rule? How does it differ from basic Timestamp Ordering?
expectedPoints: Thomas' Write Rule modifies the write condition in TO
If TS(T) < WTS(Q): Write is ignored (not rejected)
If TS(T) < RTS(Q): Write is rejected and T is rolled back
Difference: Basic TO rejects writes where TS<TWTS, while Thomas' ignores them

question: List any four advantages of Timestamp Ordering over lock-based concurrency control.
expectedPoints: No deadlocks can occur (timestamp ordering prevents circular wait)
No cascading rollbacks (each transaction reads committed data only)
Does not require blocking of transactions
Simple to implement compared to lock management

question: What is the problem of starvation in Timestamp Ordering? How can it occur?
expectedPoints: Starvation occurs when a transaction keeps getting rolled back
Long-running transactions may be repeatedly rolled back
Younger transactions keep accessing data items needed by older transaction
Older transaction never completes because its timestamp is always older than recent writes

question: Define Read Timestamp (RTS) and Write Timestamp (WTS) with respect to a data item.
expectedPoints: RTS(Q): Largest timestamp among all transactions that have successfully read data item Q
WTS(Q): Largest timestamp among all transactions that have successfully written data item Q
Both are maintained by the system for each data item
Used in read and write rules to determine if operations should proceed

question: Explain why Timestamp Ordering does not cause cascading rollbacks.
expectedPoints: Each transaction reads only committed data due to timestamp checks
Transactions work with their own copy or consistent snapshot
Rollback of one transaction does not affect other transactions
Unlike lock-based protocols where reading uncommitted data can cause cascading rollback

question: Consider the following scenario: Transaction T1 has timestamp 10, Transaction T2 has timestamp 20. Data item X has RTS=15 and WTS=18. Determine whether the following operations are allowed or rejected: (a) T1 reads X (b) T2 reads X (c) T1 writes X (d) T2 writes X. Show your reasoning.
expectedPoints: (a) T1 reads X: TS(10) < WTS(18), so REJECTED - T1 is older than the last writer
(b) T2 reads X: TS(20) >= WTS(18), so ALLOWED - T2 is newer than the last writer
(c) T1 writes X: TS(10) < RTS(15) and TS(10) < WTS(18), so REJECTED - too old for both
(d) T2 writes X: TS(20) >= RTS(15) and TS(20) >= WTS(18), so ALLOWED - newer than both reader and writer
